div.head: PE–34. Memorandum from the Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs (Rubottom) to the Deputy Coordinator for Mutual Security (Bell)Source: Department of State, Rubottom Files, Lot 61 D 279, “Peru 1959.” Confidential. Drafted by Poole and Spencer. The source text is an unsigned carbon copy.
div.p: I understand that—following the recent interagency correspondence described in Appendix ANot printed. and word of a second cruiser purchase by Peru—a draft proposed action under Circular 175,Department Circular No. 175 of December 13, 1955 set forth the procedure for the proper exercise of the treaty-making power and the executive agreement-making power of the United States, and approval of texts and the making of arrangements for signature. It was transmitted under cover of Foreign Service Circular No. 164 of January 13, 1956. Circular 175 procedure is codified in 11 Foreign Affairs Manual 70 et seq. in implementation of the Ship Loan Program as a whole, has been sent to the Defense Department that for Peru includes two MSFs (converted minesweepers) but omits the originally programmed destroyer. (See Appendix, para 4, re MSFs) The omission of the destroyer was in reaction to Peru’s second cruiser purchase and was made pending further consideration of the matter.

You are, of course, aware of our recent unsuccessful efforts to induce Peru to forego the purchase of the British cruisers, which we consider militarily unnecessary, economically wasteful and likely to contribute to a Latin American arms race.

I have very carefully considered—in the light of Peru’s purchase of the two cruisers and the cynical attitude of certain Peruvian officials in going back on Peru’s assurances to us that they would not purchase the
second cruiser—whether it would serve our interests in Peru and the hemisphere as a whole now to deny Peru the loan and rehabilitation of the Ship Loan Act destroyer earmarked for Peru in the grant military assistance program. I have concluded, for the reasons specified below, that we can find no position that would adequately justify our denial of the destroyer to Peru while to do so would greatly prejudice our relations with Peru and our ability to carry out basic constructive programs in that country. I have therefore concluded that we must implement the program as originally planned.
Our Position re Naval Vessels for Other Latin American Countries

Neither Peru nor other Latin American countries have assumed bilateral or multilateral commitments which impose limits on the size or character of military forces they may maintain for hemispheric or national defense. In the absence of any such agreement, Peru obviously has the sovereign right to determine unilaterally its military requirements.

We have not withheld, nor made known any intention to withhold, U.S. naval vessels from other Latin American countries which have acquired or are in process of acquiring naval vessels considered by us to be militarily
and economically unjustifiable. After U.S. representations to the Brazilian and British Governments had failed in 1957 to prevent Brazilian acquisition of a British aircraft carrier, we found it necessary (in connection with the maintenance of a USAF missiles tracking facility on Brazilian territory) to agree to provide Brazil with about $87 million in military equipment, including four destroyers. One of these ships was delivered this year; the others are to be delivered during the next two years. Subsequent to Argentina’s acquisition of a British carrier in 1958, we found it in our interest to comply with President Frondizi’s personal request that we help him assure continued Argentine military support for his forward-looking economic policies, which serve U.S. interests, by extending Argentina a sizable credit for the procurement of U.S. military equipment, including two submarines scheduled for delivery this fiscal year. Now Argentina is reportedly planning to purchase three British destroyers as replacements for old vessels. We imposed no penalty when Chile purchased two British destroyers (to be delivered in the near future), Colombia two Swedish destroyers or Ecuador two British frigates, and we have earmarked one ship loan vessel, or its equivalent in the case of Ecuador, for each of these countries in the FY 1960 program. Although Venezuela has acquired eleven European destroyers since 1953, we have considered it necessary in view of our special defense and military credit arrangement which bears upon the security of the Venezuelan oil fields, to approve the sale to Venezuela of one submarine and two destroyers.
Probable Consequences in Peru if we Deny Destroyers
In the light of the instances described above, Peru would be certain to regard the withdrawal or deferral of a programmed naval vessel as discriminatory and retaliatory.

Our unsuccessful efforts at dissuading the Peruvians from purchasing the British cruisers have already aroused in certain Peruvian Government and military circles a resentment over alleged U.S. interference in a sovereign Peruvian decision. Although rational elements (including Prime Minister Beltran) agree with our position, the cruiser acquisition has been nationalistically applauded in the Peruvian Congress and press. Our hopes that the purchase of the second cruiser could be forestalled by advance assurance as the destroyer loan have likewise proved fruitless. (We hear from Peruvian and UK sources that both cruisers were purchased in one deal.)

To withdraw the destroyer would jeopardize our over-all foreign policy objectives by increasing existing resentment within the Peruvian Government and military, as well as in the Congress, press, public, etc. We already have reports that the Peruvian Navy might terminate our Naval Mission, withdraw Peruvian naval personnel in the U.S. and remove pro-American naval officers from positions of influence in the Peruvian Navy. (Our Navy Department is most anxious to avoid these possibilities and
therefore is in wholehearted agreement with our position on the destroyers.) It is also possible that Prime Minister Beltran, whose pro-American views and whose opposition to excessive military expenditures are well known, might find his position untenable is the face of aroused nationalistic, anti-American feelings. The U.S. would then lose a good friend and Peru would lose a man who is providing positive leadership in developing a forward-looking economic-financial-social program. There is reason to believe that the downfall of Beltran would be a severe blow to Peruvian prospects of maintaining economic and political stability.

I believe that our unilateral attempts to discourage excessive arms purchases, as in the Peruvian ease, are severely limited by present conditions and that we must try to develop an effective general Inter-American approach to the problem.

RecommendationsThere is no indication on the source text of Bell’s action on the recommendation. In airgram A–135 to Lima, January 15, the Department of State asked the Embassy to offer Peru one destroyer and two converted minesweepers on a five-year renewable loan. (723.5621/1–1560) For further information, see the editorial note, Document PE–14.

I recommend that U/MSC take necessary action as soon as possible to: (1)
Advise the Budget Bureau (See Appendix, para. 1), either directly or through the Defense Department, that it will be necessary for foreign policy reasons to implement the Peruvian Ship Loan Program as originally planned. (2)
Include one destroyer for Peru (in addition to the two MSFs) in the Circular 175 memorandum which has been transmitted to the Defense Department. (In order that the final U.S. decision regarding the FY 1960 Ship Loan Program may be conveyed concurrently to Peru and all other participating countries, it is essential that Circular 175 approval for the complete Latin American Ship Loan Program be obtained in one single memorandum.)
